notes.note: Arsaces is the dynastic name of the Parthian kings and Seleucia-on-the-Tigris a chief seat of their power; Caelius means that no eastern conquest by Cicero would be worth missing the political theatre at Rome.

notes.anchor: the augurate, of which I had been the promoter
notes.note: The vacant place in the college of augurs went to Antony, whom Caelius had backed, over L. Domitius Ahenobarbus; Domitius blamed Caelius for the humiliating defeat.
Sextus Peducaeus had just been acquitted, which Caelius reads as a favourable omen for the coming prosecution of young Cn. Saturninus.
The jurors and the propertied classes who staffed the courts (qui res iudicant); Caelius predicts they will side with Pompey and the Senate against Caesar.

notes.anchor: Appius our censor is producing prodigies
notes.note: As censor in 50 BC Appius Claudius Pulcher conducted a severe purge of senatorial morals; Caelius mocks his zeal over luxury-goods (statues, pictures), property assessment, and debt.

notes.anchor: the Scantinia-law case at Drusus's bench
notes.note: The trial under the Lex Scantinia (against unnatural offences) was heard before the praetor M. Livius Drusus Claudianus; Caelius savours the spectacle of the puritanical censor Appius himself being prosecuted under it.

notes.anchor: his concession on the matter of the pay for Pompey
notes.note: Curio is praised for yielding (remisit de stipendio) over the funds or pay claimed for Pompey's troops, a tactical retreat that strengthened his position against the optimates.
